---
title: "Authoring a widget: write ESM once, run it everywhere"
---

This page is for **authoring a new widget**, not porting a backend (for that, see
[porting guide](../extending/porting.md)). The core cositos promise, delivered today: you
write one small [ESM](glossary.qmd) module and it renders unchanged in the browser, in a
live Jupyter kernel, and in a static export — **no new JavaScript per host**.

> **Unfamiliar terms?** *Model*, *trait*, *reducer*, *AnyModel* are all defined in the
> [Glossary](glossary.qmd). You need to read/write a little JavaScript to author a widget
> (the ESM `render` function); using a pre-built widget requires none.

<!-- Repo-relative paths to files outside docs/ are plain text here, not markdown
     links: docs/tutorials/*.qmd renders one directory level deeper than its source,
     so a relative link outside docs/ 404s in the built site. See cositos-7co. -->

## The minimal ESM shape

A cositos widget is a plain anywidget-style ES module: a default export with a
`render({ model, el })` function. `examples/widgets/button.js` is a complete, minimal
example:

```js
// Button — button/event category (ipywidgets: Button). Uses a custom message to notify
// the kernel of clicks (ipywidgets Button emits an "on_click" event, not state).
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const button = document.createElement("button");
    const paint = () => {
      button.textContent = model.get("description") ?? "Click";
    };
    paint();
    button.addEventListener("click", () => {
      model.set("clicks", (model.get("clicks") ?? 0) + 1);
      model.save_changes();
      model.send({ event: "click" });
    });
    model.on("change:description", paint);
    el.appendChild(button);
    return () => button.remove(); // optional cleanup, called on unmount
  },
};
```

The `model` passed into `render` is the same `Model` surface anywidget gives you:
`get(key)` / `set(key, value)` / `on("change:key", cb)` / `off` / `save_changes()` /
`send(content)` / `request_state()`. This is a fixed contract regardless of host — see
`hosts.md` for the full `Channel`/`Model` seam that makes that possible.

## Where widgets live, and what patterns are already covered

`examples/widgets/*.js` holds seven small, render+interaction-tested widgets covering the
six ipywidgets synchronization patterns (two-way state, kernel→view push, dynamic option
lists, custom events). Before writing a new pattern from scratch, check whether yours is
a variant of one of these — see the category table in `widgets.md`.

## The same file, three hosts, no changes

| Host | What you need | Worked example |
|---|---|---|
| Browser, no kernel | `Model` + `LocalChannel` from `@cositos/front` | below |
| Live Jupyter kernel | a `Widget` façade + a `Transport` (Python `CommTransport`, Julia `CositosIJuliaExt`) | `tutorials/quickstart.qmd` |
| Static export, no kernel | `dump_document` + `embed_html`/`write_html` | `tutorials/static-export.qmd` |

The browser path needs no kernel and no build step, so it is the fastest way to see a
widget you're authoring render, before wiring it into a kernel at all:

```js
import { Model, LocalChannel, loadWidget, renderWidget } from "../../front/src/index.js";

const esm = await (await fetch("./my_widget.js")).text();
const model = new Model({ value: 0 }, new LocalChannel());
const widget = await loadWidget(esm);
await renderWidget(widget, { model, el: document.getElementById("mount") });
```

Serve the folder over HTTP (`python3 -m http.server`) rather than opening the file
directly — ES module imports need `http://`, not `file://`. The full runnable version,
including a `LocalChannel` reducer for client-side-only logic, is
`examples/web/index.html`.

## Before you compose widgets: two caveats

If your widget is a container that holds *other* widgets (a `VBox`-style layout, a
dashboard), two things surprise first-time authors — read this before writing a
composition example, not after debugging one:

1. **A plain cositos widget cannot hold live widget references.** `AnyModel` (what
   every cositos widget emits) declares no widget-reference traits, so an
   `"IPY_MODEL_<id>"` string in your state — e.g. a `children` list — reaches the
   frontend as a literal string, not a resolved child widget. Composition needs a real
   ipywidgets **controls** container (`VBoxModel` + `LayoutModel`, not `AnyModel`) as
   the parent; see the "Composing widgets" section of `tutorials/static-export.qmd` for
   the full recipe and a runnable reference (`examples/composition/build.py`).
2. **`jslink`/`dlink` do not work in a static (backend-less) export.** Linking two
   widgets creates a `LinkModel` that only gets instantiated when a live kernel's
   html-manager references it; in a static export there's no kernel to do that, so
   link updates silently don't propagate. Linking requires a live kernel.

## Next steps

- Drive your widget from a live kernel: `tutorials/quickstart.qmd`.
- Ship it in a saved/static document: `tutorials/static-export.qmd`.
- Embed it into an existing tool (Quarto, JupyterBook, Voila, Clay, …):
  `tutorials/integrating.qmd`.
